jokes.text: Even Sweet Dee's getting lucky! That dude's gonna bang your sister, bro.
jokes.setup: Characters celebrating how well the night is going at their bar
jokes.punchline: Crude observation about Dee's romantic prospects delivered casually
jokes.explanation: Establishes the show's crude, inappropriate tone early. The casual vulgarity about a sister is both shocking and character-revealing.

jokes.text: How old are you? Twenty-one? Everybody, out!
jokes.setup: Characters noticing the crowd seems young, casually asking someone's age
jokes.punchline: Immediate panic reaction when they realize they're serving minors
jokes.explanation: Great comedic timing with the abrupt shift from casual questioning to immediate panic. Economical and effective.

jokes.text: Everybody, out!
jokes.setup: Realizing they're serving minors after the weak 'twenty-one' claim
jokes.punchline: Immediate panic and mass ejection
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the setup - the abrupt shift from celebration to panic works well structurally.

jokes.text: And we also have a social responsibility to keep teenagers from drinking. I guess.
jokes.setup: Dennis making a responsible statement about social duty
jokes.punchline: Charlie's completely unenthusiastic 'I guess' undermines any moral authority
jokes.explanation: Shows the characters' complete lack of genuine moral concern. Charlie's dismissive response perfectly deflates Dennis's attempt at righteousness.

jokes.text: We would almost get raped and/or murdered and/or stabbed by crackheads in Fairmount Park.
jokes.setup: Mac explaining what happened when they got kicked out of bars as teens
jokes.punchline: Casually lists extremely violent potential outcomes as if they're minor inconveniences
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy - treating serious violent crimes as casual inconveniences. The 'and/or' construction adds absurdist precision to horrific scenarios.

jokes.text: Every time some guy talks to you that you like, you get so nervous you drink yourself into oblivion. I had bad potato salad.
jokes.setup: Mac calling out Dee's pattern of drinking when nervous around guys
jokes.punchline: Dee's obviously false excuse about potato salad
jokes.explanation: Classic character comedy showing Dee's inability to admit her flaws. The potato salad excuse is absurdly transparent.

jokes.text: Maybe we have 'a social responsibility' to provide a safe haven for these kids to be kids.
jokes.setup: Mac twisting the earlier moral argument
jokes.punchline: Using air quotes around 'social responsibility' to justify serving minors
jokes.explanation: Shows the characters' ability to rationalize anything. The air quotes (implied) around 'social responsibility' reveal their cynicism.

jokes.text: I don't know how you guys live with yourselves. One day at a time. One day at a time.
jokes.setup: Dee expressing disgust at their moral compromise
jokes.punchline: Mac and Charlie's simultaneously delivered, clichéd recovery wisdom
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan delivery of AA-style wisdom in response to moral criticism. The synchronized delivery makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: As a matter of fact, I have a date this afternoon with my friend Steven.
jokes.setup: Dee arguing with her mother on the phone, trying to sound mature
jokes.punchline: Revealing her 'date' is with someone named Steven (likely a teenager)
jokes.explanation: Sets up Dee's inappropriate relationship storyline. The formal 'friend Steven' sounds suspiciously like she's trying to convince herself it's innocent.

jokes.text: I've been there, like, 200 times.
jokes.setup: Trey mentions Lemon Hill as where 'cool kids' go
jokes.punchline: Dee's obvious lie about being familiar with the teen hangout spot
jokes.explanation: Dee's transparent attempt to seem cool and experienced. The specific number '200' makes the lie more ridiculous.

jokes.text: The trick is to just kind of open your throat.
jokes.setup: Trey commenting on how much Dee can drink
jokes.punchline: Dee giving drinking advice that sounds sexual and inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Double entendre that reveals Dee's inappropriate behavior with a minor. The phrasing is both crude and reveals her desperation to impress.

jokes.text: The only reason you got to hang out with me and the other cool kids is 'cause you sold us all weed. Everybody thought you were an asshole.
jokes.setup: Mac claiming he was popular in high school
jokes.punchline: Dennis revealing Mac was only tolerated because he was the drug dealer
jokes.explanation: Great character work destroying Mac's delusions. Shows how the gang maintains their toxic dynamic through brutal honesty.

jokes.text: You were popular like a clown is popular. All the guys knew you couldn't sleep with their girlfriends.
jokes.setup: Continuing to destroy Mac's high school popularity claims
jokes.punchline: Comparing Mac to a clown and revealing he was seen as completely non-threatening to relationships
jokes.explanation: Devastating and specific insult that perfectly captures Mac's pathetic high school status. The clown comparison is both specific and emasculating.

jokes.text: Tim Murphy slept with your prom date! Oh. He's getting sad now. Oh, no. There's, like, watering in the eyes. Now he's running away.
jokes.setup: Dennis delivering a devastating revelation about Charlie's prom
jokes.punchline: The gang's clinical observation of Charlie's emotional breakdown in real time
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cruelty where the gang treats Charlie's emotional pain like a nature documentary. The clinical descriptions of his breakdown are both funny and horrifying.

jokes.text: I never statutory raped anyone before.
jokes.setup: Dee and Trey about to get physical
jokes.punchline: Dee casually using legal terminology like it's a new hobby to try
jokes.explanation: Shocking and perfectly delivered dark comedy. Treating statutory rape as a casual first-time experience shows Dee's complete moral blindness.

jokes.text: Let's, uh, just take it slow. Where were you when I was in high school? I was eight.
jokes.setup: Dee trying to be romantic and nostalgic
jokes.punchline: Trey's matter-of-fact response revealing the disturbing age gap
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy construction. The innocent question gets the most disturbing possible answer delivered with complete casualness. Economical and devastating.

jokes.text: These kids are wasted, bro. I thought we were cuttin' 'em off. I am cuttin' 'em off. These kids haven't had more than three drinks each. Plus, there's so much water, they're probably more hydrated than they ever have been.
jokes.setup: Mac observing that the kids seem very drunk despite their plan
jokes.punchline: Dennis's absurd logic that watered-down drinks are actually healthy
jokes.explanation: Shows Dennis's ability to rationalize anything. The hydration argument is absurd but delivered with complete confidence.

jokes.text: Are they fakin' it? I guess.
jokes.setup: Kids acting very drunk on watered-down drinks
jokes.punchline: The realization that teenagers are performing drunkenness
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about teenage behavior. They're so eager to seem drunk they'll fake it even on weak drinks.

jokes.text: Charlie, that was the coolest thing you've ever done! I know. I'm gonna be sick.
jokes.setup: Mac praising Charlie for handling the fight
jokes.punchline: Charlie's immediate physical reaction undermining the 'cool' moment
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat showing Charlie's anxiety even in moments of success. The immediate deflation is very Charlie.

jokes.text: You wore that scoliosis back brace until you were 20 years old. Everyone was afraid of you. You looked like a monster!
jokes.setup: Dee claiming she was popular with guys in high school
jokes.punchline: Dennis's brutal description of her medical device making her frightening
jokes.explanation: Brutal sibling cruelty that explains Dee's current desperation. The 'monster' description is harsh but perfectly in character for Dennis.

jokes.text: I'm telling you, this is the wrong kind of glue, Charlie. This is made for, like, kindergartners or something. We don't want safe. We want toxic.
jokes.setup: Mac and Charlie trying to get high by sniffing glue
jokes.punchline: Mac's disappointed that the glue is too safe for effective huffing
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark absurdist humor. Mac's consumer complaint about safety features preventing drug abuse is wonderfully twisted.

jokes.text: Kind of a lame party, bro. Uh, what do you think? Should we jet? Yeah. This doesn't feel right.
jokes.setup: Mac and Charlie at a high school party
jokes.punchline: Grown men trying to sound cool at a teenage party while having moral qualms
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing grown men trying to fit in with teenagers. Their slang attempts and moral discomfort clash perfectly.

jokes.text: Seventy-seven seconds! That is a new record, baby!
jokes.setup: Someone doing a keg stand at the teenage party
jokes.punchline: Adult treating teenage drinking milestone as a major achievement
jokes.explanation: Cringe comedy about adults taking teenage party games too seriously. The pride in the 'record' shows their immaturity.

jokes.text: Yeah! What do you think about that, bitches? Load 'em up, losers! What do you think about this, huh? How I embarrassed your boyfriend in front of everybody?
jokes.setup: Mac apparently winning at beer pong or similar
jokes.punchline: Aggressively trash-talking teenagers like it's a serious competition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Mac behavior - taking teenage party games way too seriously and being aggressively competitive with kids.

jokes.text: What's with this guy? I know. What is he, like, 40?
jokes.setup: Mac's aggressive behavior at the party
jokes.punchline: Teenagers accurately identifying that he's pathetically old to be there
jokes.explanation: Good reality check moment where the teens see through the gang's delusions. The age guess is hilariously close to accurate.

jokes.text: Trey asked me to prom last night. This is getting really weird. That girl Sara asked me too.
jokes.setup: The gang getting more involved with high schoolers
jokes.punchline: Multiple prom invitations revealing how inappropriate things have gotten
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how deep they've gotten into inappropriate relationships. The 'weird' assessment shows some self-awareness.

jokes.text: You can either take me to the prom or I can tell the police that you're serving underage kids. Are you serious? Try me.
jokes.setup: Girl wanting Dennis to take her to prom
jokes.punchline: Casual blackmail threat from a high schooler
jokes.explanation: Great dark comedy showing teenagers as manipulative as the adults. The casual blackmail delivery is perfectly threatening.

jokes.text: The only reason that Sara asked you in the first place is because you're the only idiot dumb enough to listen to her bitch about her ex-boyfriend all night!
jokes.setup: Mac and Charlie arguing about the prom invitations
jokes.punchline: Mac's brutal but accurate assessment of why Charlie was chosen
jokes.explanation: Good character insight showing Charlie was chosen for his capacity to listen, not his attractiveness. Mac's brutal honesty is typically hurtful.

jokes.text: My back brace was under the dress, and Mom called me fat. So I stayed home and cried instead.
jokes.setup: Explaining why she didn't go to prom
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation of a traumatic teenage experience
jokes.explanation: Good character backstory that explains her current desperation. The casual delivery of trauma is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: That dude is going to bang your sister, bro.
jokes.setup: Trey arriving to pick up Dee
jokes.punchline: Charlie repeating the line from the opening, now with different context
jokes.explanation: Good callback that gains meaning from the episode's events. Now we know Trey is a teenager, making the line more disturbing.

jokes.text: Yes, and your eyebrows are drawn on, so... Say it, don't spray it.
jokes.setup: Mac and Dee trading insults
jokes.punchline: Mac's weak comeback followed by Dee's elementary school-level retort
jokes.explanation: Weak jokes that show the characters' limited insult abilities. Both responses are childish and unoriginal.

jokes.text: He's totally gonna bang your sister.
jokes.setup: Trey and Dee leaving together
jokes.punchline: Mac repeating the crude observation again
jokes.explanation: Another callback to the running line. Gets slightly less funny each time but maintains the crude tone.

jokes.text: I always wanted to have sex on prom.
jokes.setup: Trey and Dee in romantic moment
jokes.punchline: Teenage boy's blunt sexual agenda
jokes.explanation: Shows the teenager's simple motivations. The direct approach contrasts with Dee's romantic delusions.

jokes.text: Eighteen's legal, bro. This isn't a morality contest. Eighteen's not young, brother.
jokes.setup: Dennis justifying his 18-year-old date after criticizing the others
jokes.punchline: His obviously hypocritical rationalization
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dennis hypocrisy - establishing rules for others but exempting himself. The mental gymnastics are transparently self-serving.

jokes.text: They were actually using you two to make each other jealous.
jokes.setup: Trey and his ex-girlfriend leaving together
jokes.punchline: Charlie explaining the obvious manipulation they fell for
jokes.explanation: Good revelation showing how easily the adults were manipulated by teenagers. Charlie's matter-of-fact explanation adds to the embarrassment.

jokes.text: That dude's gonna bang your prom date, bro.
jokes.setup: Trey leaving with his ex instead of Dee
jokes.punchline: Final use of the running line, now directed at Dennis
jokes.explanation: Final callback to the running line, now with maximum irony since Dennis was the one judging others. The repetition has built to this payoff.

jokes.text: Just like high school.
jokes.setup: The gang fighting and humiliated after the prom disaster
jokes.punchline: Charlie's observation that they're recreating their teenage dynamics
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending line that ties together the episode's theme. Shows they haven't grown at all since high school, despite their age.
